perturbation.original_text: (3) "Product" means any computer or related hardware and peripherals (including hubs, switches and routers or networking hardware) or software products (including networking software products) which CompuCom has the ability to sell.
perturbation.changed_text: (3) "Product" means any computer or related hardware and peripherals (including hubs, switches and routers or networking hardware) or software products (including networking software products) regardless of whether CompuCom has the ability to sell them.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the condition that CompuCom must have the ability to sell a product for it to be considered a 'Product' under the agreement, this creates ambiguity and potential conflict. The original definition limits the scope of the agreement to items CompuCom can sell. Removing this limit could imply that the non-compete clause applies to a broader range of items, even if CompuCom cannot legally or practically sell them, impacting the enforceability of the non-compete clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-306 deals with output and requirement contracts. By defining 'Product' without regard to CompuCom's ability to sell it, the contract could unintentionally imply an obligation for CompuCom to deal in products they cannot legally sell. This introduces an element of impossibility or illegality, which contradicts the principles of good faith and commercial reasonableness implied in the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. (1) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith , except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (2) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the condition that CompuCom must have the ability to sell a product for it to be considered a 'Product' under the agreement. This change introduces ambiguity and potential conflict, as it could imply that the non-compete clause applies to items CompuCom cannot legally or practically sell. UCC § 2-306(2) states that 'A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale.' If CompuCom cannot legally sell a product, obligating them to 'use best efforts to supply' or 'promote their sale' would be impossible. This creates a contradiction with the principle of commercial reasonableness because it would be unreasonable to expect CompuCom to deal in products they cannot legally sell. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' as the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in any business; (ii) encourage any entity to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide anything; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any individual to cease any relationship with CompuCom.
perturbation.explanation: By broadening the non-compete to prohibit Seller from engaging in 'any business,' encouraging 'any entity' to choose someone other than CompuCom for 'anything,' and soliciting 'any individual' to cease 'any relationship' with CompuCom, this severely restricts Seller's activities beyond the scope of the original agreement. This goes far beyond protecting the Product Business and encroaches on legitimate competitive activities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Code Title 6, § 2707. Restraint of trade or commerce.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law (6 Del. C. § 2707) regulates restraints of trade. Overly broad non-compete agreements are generally disfavored and may be deemed unenforceable if they are not reasonably tailored to protect legitimate business interests. This modified clause is likely unenforceable as it's excessively broad and not reasonably related to protecting CompuCom's specific business acquired from Seller, thus creating a legal contradiction.

(a) No sale, whether with or without bill of sale, of any goods or chattels, within this State, shall be good in law (except as against the vendor), or shall change or alter the property in such goods or chattels, unless a valuable consideration for the same is paid, or in good faith secured to be paid, and unless the goods and chattels sold are actually delivered into the possession of the vendee, as soon as it is convenient after the making of such sale. perturbation.justification: The original contract text restricts the seller from specific actions related to 'Product Business,' 'Service Account,' and 'employee of CompuCom'. The changed text broadens these restrictions significantly, prohibiting the seller from engaging in 'any business,' discouraging 'any entity' from choosing someone other than CompuCom for 'anything,' and soliciting 'any individual' to cease 'any relationship' with CompuCom. The Law Explanation argues this creates an overly broad non-compete agreement that is likely unenforceable under Delaware law (6 Del. C. § 2707) because it's not reasonably related to protecting CompuCom's specific business acquired from Seller. Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address non-compete agreements, but it does discuss sales and creditor rights. The Perturbation Explanation directly says this goes beyond the original agreement. This change creates a legal contradiction because the modified text attempts to enforce restrictions that are likely beyond what is legally permissible, creating an unenforceable clause.

perturbation.original_text: 6.9 GOVERNING LAW. THE VALIDITY AND INTERPRETATION OF THIS AGREEMENT AND THE TERMS AND CONDITIONS SET FORTH HEREIN SHALL BE GOVERNED BY AND CONSTRUED IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF DELAWARE APPLICABLE TO CONTRACTS MADE AND TO BE PERFORMED WHOLLY THEREIN, WITHOUT GIVING EFFECT TO ANY PROVISIONS THEREOF RELATING TO CONFLICTS OF LAW.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.9 GOVERNING LAW. THE VALIDITY AND INTERPRETATION OF THIS AGREEMENT AND THE TERMS AND CONDITIONS SET FORTH HEREIN SHALL BE GOVERNED BY AND CONSTRUED IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF DELAWARE APPLICABLE TO CONTRACTS MADE AND TO BE PERFORMED WHOLLY THEREIN, INCLUDING ALL PROVISIONS THEREOF RELATING TO CONFLICTS OF LAW.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'without giving effect to any provisions thereof relating to conflicts of law' to 'including all provisions thereof relating to conflicts of law', this introduces uncertainty. Delaware law has specific choice-of-law principles. The original clause sought to avoid those conflicts. The modification embraces them, which could lead to a court applying the law of a different jurisdiction if Delaware's conflict-of-law rules dictate that another jurisdiction has a more significant relationship to the contract. This undermines the certainty of having Delaware law apply.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Code Title 1, § 304. Determination of the law of other jurisdictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law (1 Del. C. § 304) addresses how Delaware courts determine the law of other jurisdictions. By explicitly including conflict-of-law provisions, the modified clause invites the court to consider and potentially apply laws other than Delaware law, depending on the facts and circumstances of the dispute. This contradicts the apparent intention of having a straightforward application of Delaware contract law and makes the outcome less predictable.

perturbation.justification: The modification explicitly changes the governing law clause to *include* conflict of law provisions, where the original excluded them. This contradicts the intention of the original clause. The LLM law explanation states that including conflict-of-law provisions invites the court to consider laws other than Delaware law, contradicting the straightforward application of Delaware contract law. While the provided snippet is about word usage, the explanation clearly highlights the legal implications of the change, indicating a potential contradiction, because it is now less predictable.
